title: Quality Control in Water Damage Restoration — Daily Moisture Logs, Photo Verification & Drying Reports
description: How to implement quality control systems in your restoration business — daily moisture monitoring logs, photo verification at each stage of work, drying progress reports, and final documentation packages for Australian insurance claims.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Quality Control in the Restoration Context

Quality control (QC) in structural drying refers to the systematic monitoring and documentation of drying progress to ensure that work meets IICRC S500:2025 standards and that deviations are identified and corrected promptly. A well-implemented QC system simultaneously produces the documentation required for insurance settlement.

QC is not just a back-office function — it is the daily activity of every technician on every monitoring visit. The technician who takes and records the moisture readings, checks the equipment is running correctly, and notes any anomalies is the front line of quality control.

## Daily Moisture Monitoring

Under IICRC S500:2025 §9.1, the restorer must monitor the drying environment daily and record:

- Air temperature and relative humidity (at minimum — GPP or specific humidity where equipment allows)
- Material moisture content at all documented grid points
- Equipment status (all units running, any failures noted)
- Observations about drying progression or issues (e.g., 'Wall cavity readings not progressing — consider injection drying at next visit')

Each day's readings should be compared to the previous day's readings to confirm that drying is progressing. A flat trend — no reduction in moisture content over two or more consecutive readings — is a trigger for intervention: equipment repositioning, additional units, or wall cavity access.

## Photo Verification at Each Stage

A robust QC photo set spans the entire job from pre-works to final:

**Pre-works photos**: Document the condition of all affected areas before any work commences. These are the baseline record.

**During-works photos**: Taken at each monitoring visit. For jobs involving demolition (material removal), photos should capture:
- Materials at the point of removal (confirming they were wet or contaminated before removal)
- Post-removal substrate (confirming the extent of affected area that has been cleared)
- Equipment placement on each visit (confirms equipment was present and positioned correctly)

**Post-works photos**: All restored and repaired areas after completion. Compared to pre-works photos to confirm the scope has been completed.

## Drying Progress Reports

A drying progress report is a summary document produced at the end of each monitoring visit. It should include:

- Job identifier and site address
- Visit date and time
- Technician name and qualifications
- Current temperature, RH, and GPP readings (ambient)
- Summary of moisture readings at all grid points (compared to previous visit)
- Equipment inventory (units on-site, operating status)
- Drying progress assessment: on track / intervention required
- Any changes made (equipment repositioning, additional units, wall cavity access)
- Next visit scheduled date

The series of drying progress reports constitutes the drying log — the day-by-day record of the drying process from commencement to drying goal achievement.

## The Final Documentation Package

The complete documentation package submitted at invoice stage should include:

1. Initial inspection report (category, class, initial moisture map, damage photos)
2. Scope of works (line items with IICRC justification)
3. Drying log (all daily progress reports)
4. Drying completion certificate (confirming drying goal achieved on two consecutive days)
5. Final post-works photo set
6. Equipment log (daily hire records for all equipment)
7. Any specialist reports (hygienist clearance, asbestos, engineering)

A complete documentation package submitted with the invoice significantly reduces the likelihood of queries and payment delays.
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today we're talking about quality control in structural drying — the day-to-day monitoring and documentation systems that protect your business and get your invoices paid.

Quality control isn't a back-office task. It's what happens on every monitoring visit: moisture readings, equipment checks, and observations about what's changing. The daily monitoring visit is the most important quality control activity on any active drying job.

Under IICRC S500:2025 section 9.1, daily monitoring must record air temperature and relative humidity, material moisture content at all documented grid points, equipment status, and any relevant observations. Compare each day's readings to the previous day. If the moisture content is flat — no reduction over two consecutive readings — that's a trigger for intervention. Reposition equipment, add units, or consider accessing wall cavities.

Photos through the job are part of quality control too. You need pre-works photos of everything before you start — that's your baseline. During-works photos at each visit, particularly at the point of material removal. I want to see the wet, damaged material before it comes out, and I want to see the substrate after it's gone. Those photos confirm that the removal was justified and that the work was done. Post-works photos at completion confirm the scope has been executed.

At each monitoring visit, produce a brief drying progress report. Job details, date, temperature and humidity readings, moisture reading summary, equipment status, and an assessment of whether you're on track or whether intervention is needed. The series of these reports is your drying log — the day-by-day record of the job.

At the end of the job, your documentation package for the insurer should include: the initial inspection report, the scope of works, the complete drying log, the drying completion certificate, the final photo set, the equipment hire log, and any specialist reports. That's everything the adjuster needs to process the claim without coming back to you for more information.

The most common reason invoices get queried is incomplete documentation — not because the work wasn't done, but because the record doesn't prove it was done. Fix that with a systematic QC process.

RestoreAssist structures all of this documentation automatically as you work through the inspection and daily monitoring workflow.

Head to restoreassist.com.au.
